topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the soft, damning finality of a gavel strike. "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise party planned for Saturday has been cancelled." My fingers, hovering over the keyboard, froze mid-air. I reread the message three times, the words swimming before my eyes. A slow, cold dread began to coil in my stomach, tightening with every repetition. I stared blankly at the brightly colored invitation mock-up pinned to my corkboard—the balloons, the coordinated theme—now seeming garish and mocking. A sharp intake of breath escaped my lips, and I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white. Who would even know? The silence of the room suddenly felt cavernous, amplifying the frantic drumming of my pulse against my ribs.

[story 2]

I walked into the living room, the silence of the house pressing in on me like thick velvet. The remnants of the decorations—a half-inflated banner, a forgotten string of fairy lights—seemed to mock the sudden void where celebration should have been. My gaze snapped to my phone screen. The text was stark: 'Party off. Everything is cancelled.' A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim slightly. I dropped the phone onto the sofa cushion, the sound muffled by the upholstery. I stood motionless for a long moment, just breathing in shallow, quick gasps. My hands began to tremble uncontrollably, and I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to scrub the sight of the cancellation from my mind, but the words refused to dissipate.

[story 3]

When I saw the notification pop up, the world tilted. It wasn't a gradual realization; it was a sudden, jarring impact, like being knocked off balance. My breath hitched, catching painfully in my throat. I stumbled backward, bumping against the wall, the plaster cool against my suddenly clammy skin. I stared at the screen, my jaw slack, unable to process the simple, devastating fact. A low, ragged sound escaped my chest—a noise halfway between a gasp and a choked sob. I started pacing, a frantic, tight circuit around the room, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. Every instinct screamed at me to deny it, to question it, but the sheer weight of the news settled deep in my chest, making every movement feel impossibly heavy.
